premise: An eighty-year-old former medium, banished from her family's traveling shrine business years ago, becomes convinced that the teenage grandson chosen to inherit it is being coached by the same hungry saint that ruined her brother. As the clan drifts from revival camp to revival camp selling miracles to the desperate, she starts sabotaging the boy's visions, unsure whether she is exposing a fraud or murdering the only real holiness left in the family.
engine_guess: aging matriarch tries to break a possible child-saint before he inherits the family trade
pressure_descriptor: succession panic, road-bound sabotage, and a possibly genuine haunting
setting_descriptor: a contemporary revival circuit run by a traveling shrine family
why_it_might_work: It promises a sharp mix of family cruelty, roadside ritual, comic hustling, and genuine metaphysical dread. The grandmother's obsession can generate both intimate scenes and escalating public fallout without leaning on bureaucratic machinery.
risk: The voice has to stay viciously funny without turning the family into caricature. If the saint question resolves too cleanly, the premise could lose the unstable charge that makes it live.

premise: Every spring, the residents of a drought commune wade into red mud pits to hand-raise bioengineered salamanders whose toxic eggs, when fermented and burned, let the settlement speak with its dead for one night. A nineteen-year-old hatchery worker begins an affair with the commune's married chant leader just as a disease tears through the brood, forcing the whole community through the filthy sequence of culling, breeding, and ritual feasting that keeps both the power grid and the afterlife open.
engine_guess: young hatchery worker caught between communal survival labor and a dangerous affair
pressure_descriptor: infected brood, erotic entanglement, and a communal rite that cannot fail
setting_descriptor: a drought-struck mud commune powered by biotech necromancy
why_it_might_work: The book has a strong novel engine in the seasonal labor itself: mud, breeding, disease control, appetite, chant, and grief all press on one another. It can move between erotic secrecy, collective argument, bodily work, and stable metaphysical stakes without defaulting to institutions.
risk: The invented biology and ritual have to feel usable rather than ornamental. If the commune becomes too uniformly earnest or too uniformly grotesque, the human drama could flatten.

premise: A captioner for immersive memorial streams loses her adult daughter in a ferry fire and begins seeing the wrong names snap onto strangers in every city she travels to for work. As bereaved crowds start asking her to identify which of the living are carrying their dead, she becomes the unwilling center of a national obsession that may be grief, fraud, or the first public collapse of stable personhood.
engine_guess: bereaved traveler becomes a public witness to unstable identity
pressure_descriptor: private bereavement turning into a civic naming crisis
setting_descriptor: near-future cities linked by memorial media and post-disaster pilgrimage
why_it_might_work: It offers an intimate tragic consciousness that can widen into shared consequence without becoming procedural. The premise gives room for travel, vigils, imposture, media frenzy, and the unsettling question of what a person is when names stop holding.
risk: The ontological instability has to stay emotionally legible, not merely abstract. If the public phenomenon overwhelms the daughter's loss, the novel could lose its center of gravity.

premise: A family of hereditary fleshwrights rides the empire's marriage train, performing the brutal sequence of cuts and grafts that lets aristocratic couples share blood, titles, and fertility before they reach the capital. When the youngest son is ordered to prepare the woman he loves for his older brother's dynastic wedding, every stop on the line tightens the timetable and the cost of refusing the work that has fed his family for generations.
engine_guess: itinerant bodycraft family trapped inside a dynastic wedding circuit
protagonist_descriptor: young fleshwright son born into a marriage-making trade
pressure_descriptor: precise surgical ritual colliding with forbidden love and family duty
setting_descriptor: an alternate historical empire aboard a nonstop marriage train
why_it_might_work: The closed moving setting gives the book a relentless structure while leaving room for ceremony, jealousy, gore, class display, and family negotiation. Romance is central, but the real engine is the repeated violence required to turn love and lineage into the same thing.
risk: It needs enough social texture beyond the central triangle to feel like a full novel rather than an ornate premise. The body modifications must be coherent and consequential, not decorative shock.

premise: When the mining company abandons the last subsidized neighborhood built inside the ribs of a dead sky-beast, the five adult children of the carcass's longtime caretaker start selling different miracles from its organs: heat from the heart chambers, luxury polymers from the gut, prophetic mold from the lungs. As the beast begins molting time in patches and rent riots spread through the district, the siblings have to decide whether the thing is dying, waking, or finally trying to evict them.
engine_guess: bickering working-class siblings monetize a mythic carcass that may be waking up
protagonist_descriptor: five adult children of a dead-god caretaker
pressure_descriptor: rent pressure, family rivalry, and a changing body-house
setting_descriptor: a historical industrial district built inside a colossal sky-beast
why_it_might_work: It has a broad, rowdy canvas with room for grotesque domestic comedy, labor, debt, urban myth, and competing family schemes. The premise is weird in a materially useful way: the body of the beast shapes class life, technology, and every argument the siblings have.
risk: With multiple protagonists, the book needs a sharp distribution of desire so the plurality feels charged rather than diffuse. The tonal balance between comic grotesquerie and genuine stakes will be delicate.

premise: In a resort town clawed out of a marsh in 1908, a fifty-year-old eel fisher supplements her catch by rubbing medicinal mud onto the naked bodies of rich pilgrims who believe the estuary can restore fertility. When a storm uncovers the bones of the saint whose name the town has been trading on, rival landowners stage a grand reburial to lock down the marsh, and she decides to use the spectacle of their piety to destroy the claim before they destroy her water.
engine_guess: older marsh worker turns a public relic spectacle into a class war over access to the estuary
protagonist_descriptor: fifty-year-old eel fisher and mud healer
pressure_descriptor: storm exposure, inheritance theater, and a working body under public scrutiny
setting_descriptor: a 1908 marsh resort built on fertility tourism and tidal labor
why_it_might_work: The premise ties tactile labor, erotic transaction, class satire, weather, and naming power to one sharply usable place. It can sustain scenes of mud work, wealthy absurdity, bodily embarrassment, public ritual, and a very concrete fight over who gets to own a landscape.
risk: The erotic charge has to stay integral to labor and status, not drift into mere color. If the relic conflict becomes too schematic, the marsh itself could lose its force as the book's real animating presence.
